abstract: This article explores the fundamental distinctions between principled and strategic nonviolent movements. While strategic nonviolence has proven to be a highly effective, pragmatic tool for toppling repressive regimes and dictatorships, it frequently falls short when the goal shifts to a deep-seated, progressive transformation of the state. This limitation stems from a core difference in values: principled nonviolence remains deeply skeptical of traditional political power and the coercive nature of the Weberian state. In contrast, strategic movements often seek to seize and utilize the state’s existing machinery—including its monopoly on force—to secure their own political ends. As evidenced by the outcomes in Egypt, Palestine, and Syria, this willingness to compromise often leads to the corruption of the movement's original ideals. This paper argues that only through the radical commitment to pacifism and emancipatory processes central to principled nonviolence can we build social, political, and economic institutions capable of truly transcending Machiavellian politics.
